heading: Figure 1
content:
Combined figure showing a comparison of order-preserve retrieval-augmented generation and long-context language models.

**(a)** This plot presents the F1 scores of different models. The Llama3.1-70B OP-RAG variants (16K, 24K, 48K) outperform the long-context models (Llama3.1-70B, GPT-40, Gemini-1.5-Pro), with the highest score being 47.25 for OP-RAG-48K. The legend differentiates between Llama3.1-70B OP-RAG and Long Context approaches.

**(b)** This plot shows the average input token count for the same models. OP-RAG models have significantly lower input token counts (e.g., 16K for OP-RAG-16K) compared to long-context models, which range from 117K to 196K tokens.
```

The legend differentiates between Llama3.1-70B OP-RAG and Long Context approaches.

heading: Figure 2
content: The figure compares Vanilla RAG with the proposed order-preserve RAG using similarity scores of document chunks. A long document is divided into 13 chunks, each with a similarity score. The top four chunks with the highest scores are selected for both methods. Vanilla RAG sorts chunks in descending order of scores, while order-preserve RAG maintains the original order of the document. Two separate sections illustrate the ordering for each method.

heading: Figure 3
content:
The figure illustrates the influence of context length on the performance of RAG across two datasets, EN.QA and EN.MC.

**(a)** The EN.QA sub-plot shows the F1 score on the y-axis against the context length in thousands (K) on the x-axis. Two models are compared: Llama3.1-8B and Llama3.1-70B. Llama3.1-70B achieves a higher F1 score overall, peaking at a shorter context length and maintaining performance as the context grows.

**(b)** The EN.MC sub-plot displays accuracy on the y-axis versus context length on the x-axis. Similar to the EN.QA plot, Llama3.1-70B starts with a higher accuracy and holds its performance across longer context lengths compared to Llama3.1-8B, which shows more fluctuation.

sub_plots.a: The EN.QA sub-plot shows the F1 score on the y-axis against the context length in thousands (K) on the x-axis. Two models are compared: Llama3.1-8B and Llama3.1-70B. Llama3.1-70B achieves a higher F1 score overall, peaking at a shorter context length and maintaining performance as the context grows.
sub_plots.b: The EN.MC sub-plot displays accuracy on the y-axis versus context length on the x-axis. Similar to the EN.QA plot, Llama3.1-70B starts with a higher accuracy and holds its performance across longer context lengths compared to Llama3.1-8B, which shows more fluctuation.

caption:
Figure 3: The influence of context length on the performance of RAG. The evaluations are conducted on En.QA and
EN.MC datasets of ∞Bench.

heading: Figure 4: Comparisons between the proposed order-preserve RAG and vanilla RAG.
content:
Figure 4 shows comparisons between order-preserve RAG and vanilla RAG across two datasets.

**(a)** This sub-plot displays the F1 score plotted against the number of retrieved chunks for EN.QA dataset. The order-preserve RAG (orange) consistently outperforms the vanilla RAG (blue), with both methods showing improvements in F1 score as more chunks are retrieved. The order-preserve RAG peaks above a score of 45.

**(b)** This sub-plot depicts accuracy versus the number of retrieved chunks for the EN.MC dataset. The order-preserve RAG also shows superior performance compared to the vanilla RAG, achieving higher accuracy values, especially notable at around 100 retrieved chunks. Both models show a peak in performance before slightly declining.

sub_plots.a: This sub-plot displays the F1 score plotted against the number of retrieved chunks for EN.QA dataset. The order-preserve RAG (orange) consistently outperforms the vanilla RAG (blue), with both methods showing improvements in F1 score as more chunks are retrieved. The order-preserve RAG peaks above a score of 45.
sub_plots.b: This sub-plot depicts accuracy versus the number of retrieved chunks for the EN.MC dataset. The order-preserve RAG also shows superior performance compared to the vanilla RAG, achieving higher accuracy values, especially notable at around 100 retrieved chunks. Both models show a peak in performance before slightly declining.
